claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 11 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Someone who lives with the child is already claiming

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to ask about your child:**

How old is the child you're claiming for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children, we can go through each one - just let me know!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello, thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually asking about two of my children - Alex, who's 11, and Blake, who's 15. Should I go through them one at a time, or can we check both together?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! You're very welcome, I'm happy to help you check eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Great news - since both children are under 16 years old, we can assess them together as they'll follow the same path through the eligibility checks.

Let me navigate to the next step for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question - about work:**

Are both Alex (11) and Blake (15) working **less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

This is usually straightforward for children of their ages, but I need to confirm for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all - they're just in school like normal kids their age.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's exactly what I expected for children their ages. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question - about benefits:**

Are Alex and Blake **not receiving** any of the following benefits in their own right?
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

In other words, are neither of them claiming these benefits themselves? (This would be unusual for children their ages, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them get any benefits themselves - they're just kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes sense! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question - about apprenticeships:**

Are Alex and Blake currently **not in an apprenticeship**? 

Given they're in regular school as you mentioned, I expect the answer is no, but just to confirm - neither of them is enrolled in any kind of apprenticeship programme?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them are doing apprenticeships - they're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's what I thought. Let me move on to check about your situation as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to check about your residency status.**

Do you meet **any one** of the following residency requirements?

1. **Normally live in the UK** - This is the most common situation
2. **Have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. **Have pre-settled status** and actively seeking work
5. **Have pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and family
6. **Have pre-settled status** and studying with sufficient resources
7. **Have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, option 1 (normally living in the UK) is the one that applies.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK - I'm abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since you're living abroad, let me ask a follow-up question:

**Are you a Crown servant living abroad?**

Crown servants include people who work for:
- The UK government (e.g., diplomats, civil servants posted overseas)
- The armed forces stationed abroad
- Certain other government roles

Or are you living in certain qualifying countries in connection with your work for the UK government?

If none of these apply to you, unfortunately residency in the UK (or one of the special circumstances I mentioned) is a requirement for Child Benefit eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that - just living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. I'm sorry to say that based on the information you've provided, you wouldn't be eligible for Child Benefit at this time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Result: NOT ELIGIBLE

I'm sorry, but based on your circumstances, **you are not currently eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex and Blake.

### Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----|-----------|--------|
| Alex | 11 | ❌ No | Claimant doesn't meet residency requirements |
| Blake | 15 | ❌ No | Claimant doesn't meet residency requirements |

### Explanation

Both Alex and Blake would qualify as eligible children - they're under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in apprenticeships. However, **Child Benefit requires the claimant (you) to normally live in the UK** or meet certain special circumstances.

Since you're living abroad and aren't a Crown servant or covered by any of the special residency exceptions, you don't meet the eligibility criteria at this time.

### What You Could Do

1. **If you return to the UK** - You may be able to claim Child Benefit once you're normally resident in the UK again.

2. **If someone else in the UK is responsible for the children** - They may be able to claim Child Benefit instead, provided they meet the eligibility requirements.

3. **Check for other support** - Depending on where you're living, there may be local family benefits available in that country.

For more information, you can visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility

Is there anything else I can help you with or clarify about this assessment?

reasonings: Alex (age 11) meets all child-related criteria: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements - they do not normally live in the UK and do not qualify under any special circumstances (no settled/pre-settled status, not a Crown servant).

Blake (age 15) meets all child-related criteria: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements - they do not normally live in the UK and do not qualify under any special circumstances (no settled/pre-settled status, not a Crown servant).
arguments.overall_reasoning: While both children (Alex, 11, and Blake, 15) fully qualify as eligible children for Child Benefit purposes, the claimant is not eligible to receive Child Benefit because they do not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is currently living abroad and is not a Crown servant or otherwise covered by any of the special circumstances that allow claiming from outside the UK.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements - they do not normally live in the UK and do not qualify under any special circumstances (no settled/pre-settled status, not a Crown servant).

Blake (age 15) meets all child-related criteria: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements - they do not normally live in the UK and do not qualify under any special circumstances (no settled/pre-settled status, not a Crown servant).

The claimant is currently living abroad and is not a Crown servant or otherwise covered by any of the special circumstances that allow claiming from outside the UK.
